  1. Rules
  2. ›
  3. How a competition works

How a competition works

A powerlifting competition is made up of three lifts. You always do them in the same order, and your best lift in each one adds up to your total.

This is a plain-English guide. The official IPF Technical Rules are the final word.

The three lifts

Everyone does the same three lifts, always in this order:

  1. Squat. You hold the bar across your shoulders, sit down until your hips drop below your knees, then stand back up.
  2. Bench press. You lie on a bench, lower the bar to your chest, hold it still, then push it back up.
  3. Deadlift. You lift the bar from the floor until you are standing up straight.

Your attempts

You get three attempts at each lift. You choose the weight for each one, and you can go heavier each time.

Your best good lift in each of the three counts. Add those three together and you have your total.

How you place

Your total decides where you place in your category. The highest total wins.

If two lifters get the same total, the lighter lifter places higher. If they also weigh the same, the one who reached the total first places higher.

Best Lifter

Some competitions give a Best Lifter award. This uses a points formula that compares totals fairly across different bodyweights, so the strongest performance pound-for-pound can win, not just the heaviest total.

Classic and equipped

Most of our lifters compete classic (also called raw): you wear a singlet, and you may use a belt, knee sleeves and wrist wraps.

Equipped lifting allows extra supportive kit, such as a squat suit and knee wraps, which help you lift more. You enter one or the other, not both in the same category.

Next: the squat
